Mental Health Clinic in Louisville, KY

Counseling, EMDR, family therapy, case management, and community support in Louisville with clear next steps for getting started.

Reach Out
Call (502) 416-1416

How to start care

Reach out with a brief description of what is happening. The office can help sort fit, availability, insurance, and the most practical next step.

  • Send the contact form or call with a brief description of what is happening.
  • The office reviews fit, availability, insurance, and care setting needs.
  • You are routed toward the service or provider path that makes the most sense.
  • Schedule with your provider.

Services for individuals, children, couples, and families

You do not have to diagnose the problem before reaching out. Start with what is happening now, then compare counseling, family support, court-related services, community-based care, group therapy, and trauma therapy from one organized starting point.

Individual Counseling

One-on-one therapy for adults and adolescents who need steady support for anxiety, depression, trauma, stress, relationships, or major life changes.

View Page

Child Counseling

Therapy for children and teens who need help with emotional regulation, behavior, trauma, family stress, school pressure, or developmental transitions.

View Page

Family Therapy

Therapy for families who need better communication, steadier routines, healthier boundaries, and support moving through conflict or change together.

View Page

Couples Counseling

Therapy for couples who want help with communication, recurring conflict, emotional distance, trust, repair, or decisions about the relationship.

View Page

Court-Ordered Services

Support for families dealing with reunification, co-parenting needs, court expectations, documentation questions, and emotionally demanding legal transitions.

View Page

Community-Based Services

Practical mental health support for clients and families who need care coordination, community support, in-home help, and follow-through outside a traditional office visit.

Group Therapy

Structured therapy in a group setting for clients who may benefit from guided discussion, shared learning, skill practice, and connection with others working on similar concerns.

View Page

Trauma Therapy

Trauma-focused support for adults, teens, and families working through distressing memories, triggers, grief, anxiety, PTSD symptoms, or experiences that still feel active.

View Page

Use the contact path to ask about fit, availability, insurance, and the next step that makes sense for your situation.

Teen Therapy

Teen therapy for mood symptoms, trauma, identity stress, school pressure, social strain, and family conflict.

Reach Out

What To Expect In Teen Counseling

Start by describing what has changed for your teen, what school or home stress looks like, and whether individual, family, or child-focused support seems closest to the need.

Therapy for Teens

Teen years can bring fast changes in identity, relationships, independence, stress tolerance, and family roles. Therapy helps adolescents make sense of what they are experiencing and respond more effectively.

Common Reasons Families Reach Out

Families often seek adolescent counseling for anxiety, depression, family conflict, trauma, social strain, emotional shutdown, school stress, or persistent overwhelm.

School, Mood, and Family Stress

We work with teens across Louisville, Jefferson County, Bullitt County, Oldham County, and nearby Southern Indiana who are balancing school demands, family stress, and social pressure.

Can teens have private space in counseling?

Adult counseling for adults dealing with stress, anxiety, depression, grief, burnout, relationship strain, trauma, or major life changes.

What To Expect In Adult Counseling

You can begin by describing what feels hardest to manage, what kind of appointment is realistic, and whether office-based or telehealth care would fit.

Counseling for Adults

Adult counseling gives you a place to sort through what is happening now without needing the perfect label for it. Sessions may focus on work pressure, family demands, loss, anxiety, depression, trauma, burnout, relationship strain, or patterns that keep repeating.

When Adult Counseling May Help

Counseling may help when you feel stretched thin, stuck, overwhelmed, disconnected, or ready to understand why the same pressure keeps building.

Life Transitions and Relationship Strain

Adults can ask about in-person counseling at the Louisville office or telehealth when it is clinically appropriate and available. The office can also talk through insurance, fit, and whether another LMHG service should be part of the plan.

Therapy for Anxiety and Depression

Anxiety and depression often overlap. Therapy can help identify patterns, triggers, symptoms, avoidance loops, and daily pressures while building a plan for steadier routines and emotional regulation.

Who This Service Is For

This service may fit if racing thoughts, panic, low energy, hopelessness, irritability, numbness, or everyday responsibilities have started to feel bigger than your ability to keep up.

Panic, Worry, Sadness, and Burnout

We support people across Louisville and nearby counties when work pressure, family stress, school demands, financial strain, or relationship conflict intensify anxiety and depression.

routes.postContent:
Family Therapy vs. Individual Therapy | Which Fits?

Family vs individual therapy is not a competition between two choices. They answer different questions. Individual counseling focuses on one person's symptoms, stress, history, coping skills, and goals. Family therapy focuses on the patterns between people, including communication, conflict, parenting stress, trust, and shared routines.

The best starting point depends on where the problem is showing up most clearly. Some people need one private therapy space first. Some families need to work on the home pattern together. In some situations, both approaches can support the same larger goal.

Start With Individual Therapy When The Concern Is Mostly Internal

Individual therapy may be the better starting point when the main concern is anxiety, depression, trauma, grief, stress, mood changes, or a personal transition that feels hard to carry.

It can also help when you need a private place to understand your own reactions before involving other people. A therapist can help you name patterns, build coping tools, and decide what conversations or changes may need to happen next.

Start With Family Therapy When The Pattern Is Between People

Family therapy may be a better fit when the concern lives inside the family system. That can include repeated arguments, emotional distance, parenting strain, child behavior concerns, reunification stress, caregiver burnout, or communication that breaks down quickly.

If one person improves but the home pattern stays the same, progress may be hard to sustain. Family therapy gives the system a place to practice different responses together.

Child Or Teen Concerns May Need Both Views

When a child or teen is struggling, the right path may include both individual support and family involvement. A young person may need space to talk, and caregivers may need help understanding behavior, emotional regulation, school pressure, or family stress.

This does not mean anyone is to blame. It means the treatment plan should match the way the concern is actually showing up.

A Practical Way To Decide

Ask where the main difficulty seems to live right now:

  • Is it mostly inside one person, such as mood, anxiety, grief, trauma, or stress?
  • Is it mostly between people, such as conflict, disconnection, parenting strain, or family roles?
  • Are both happening at the same time?

A provider can help you sort through that question and decide whether individual therapy, family therapy, or a combination makes the most sense.

The Next Step

You do not need to choose perfectly before reaching out. If the concern involves both personal symptoms and family stress, explain both. Louisville Mental Health Group can help you compare individual counseling, family therapy, child counseling, parenting support, and other service paths before care begins.

routes.postContent:
Guide to Individual Therapy | What to Expect

Individual therapy is a one-on-one service for people who want help with anxiety, depression, trauma, grief, stress, relationship patterns, life transitions, or emotional overwhelm. It gives you a private place to understand what is happening and practice a more workable response.

You do not need to arrive with perfect language for the problem. Many people begin with a simple sentence: "I am not managing this the way I want to."

What Individual Therapy Is

Individual therapy is a confidential process with a licensed mental health provider. Sessions focus on your symptoms, experiences, goals, relationships, coping patterns, and the pressures that are most active in your life.

The work may include talking through recent stress, understanding older patterns, learning coping skills, processing trauma or grief, strengthening communication, or deciding what needs to change next.

What Usually Happens In Sessions

The first few sessions usually focus on understanding what brings you to therapy, what has already been tried, and what would make care feel useful. Sessions often last about 45 to 60 minutes, with frequency depending on clinical need, availability, and provider recommendation.

During therapy, you may:

  • Check in on recent stress, symptoms, and progress.
  • Explore thoughts, emotions, behaviors, and relationship patterns.
  • Learn coping tools or communication strategies.
  • Identify goals that are specific enough to guide the work.
  • Talk about what is helping and what needs adjustment.

When Individual Therapy May Help

Individual therapy may be worth considering when anxiety, depression, stress, grief, trauma, relationship strain, or life transitions begin affecting how you sleep, work, parent, communicate, or care for yourself.

It can also help before a crisis. Many people start because they feel stuck, tired, disconnected, or ready for support before the situation gets worse.

How To Choose A Good Fit

Provider fit matters. Consider what concerns you want help with, whether you prefer in-person care or telehealth, what insurance questions need to be answered, and whether you want a provider with experience in trauma, mood symptoms, grief, parenting, or relationships.

The right fit should feel clinically appropriate and emotionally workable. It does not have to feel perfect after one conversation, but you should have a clear sense of the next step.

How To Get Started

routes.postContent:
How to Talk to Someone About Therapy

Talking about therapy can be part of starting care, but telling people close to you may still feel uncomfortable. A partner, parent, sibling, friend, or adult child may react with worry, defensiveness, silence, or questions you were not prepared to answer.

The goal is not to convince everyone immediately. The goal is to communicate clearly, protect your decision, and invite support where it is healthy to do so.

Know Why You Are Starting

Before the conversation, get clear on your own reason. You might be seeking help for anxiety, depression, grief, relationship stress, family conflict, trauma, parenting pressure, or simply feeling stuck.

Try finishing these sentences privately:

  • "I am starting therapy because..."
  • "What I hope to work on is..."
  • "The support I need from you is..."

Clear language helps the conversation stay grounded instead of becoming an argument about whether therapy is necessary.

Choose A Calmer Moment

Avoid bringing it up during an argument, right before work, or when everyone is distracted. Choose a private moment when there is enough time to speak without rushing.

A walk, a car ride, or a quiet evening conversation may work better than a high-pressure family discussion.

Use Direct, Non-Blaming Language

You can explain your decision without making someone else responsible for it.

Instead of: "You are the reason I need therapy."

Try: "I have been carrying more stress than I know how to manage, and I want support handling it in a healthier way."

Instead of: "Our family is the problem."

Try: "Some family patterns have been hard for me, and I want help understanding how to respond more clearly."

Be Ready For Questions

Loved ones may ask whether you are okay, whether therapy means something serious happened, or whether they did something wrong. You can answer briefly and repeat the main point.

Examples:

  • "I am safe. I am doing this because I want support before things get worse."
  • "This is about taking care of my mental health, not blaming anyone."
  • "I may share more later, but I am not ready to talk through every detail right now."

Set Boundaries If The Response Is Not Helpful

Some people minimize therapy, ask intrusive questions, or respond with guilt. You can stay respectful without giving up your boundary.

You might say, "I understand this may feel unfamiliar, but I have made this decision and I am asking you to respect it."

Invite Support Without Pressure

If the person is safe and supportive, tell them what would help. That might be encouragement, privacy, transportation help, childcare, or simply not turning therapy into a debate.

If the concern involves the relationship or family system, family therapy or couples counseling may eventually be worth discussing. That conversation does not have to happen all at once.

A Practical Next Step

If you are ready to start care in the Louisville area, Louisville Mental Health Group can help you compare individual counseling, family therapy, couples counseling, and other support options. You can begin with what is happening now and let the next step become clearer from there.

routes.postContent:
When to Seek Therapy | 5 Signs Support May Help

Therapy may be worth considering when emotional strain starts affecting your sleep, work, school, parenting, relationships, routines, or ability to feel like yourself. You do not have to wait for a crisis.

Many people reach out because the problem has been building slowly. They are still functioning in some areas, but life takes more effort than it should.

These therapy signs do not mean you have failed. They mean it may be time to ask whether support could make the next step clearer.

1. Anxiety, Sadness, Or Irritability Is Staying Around

Persistent anxiety, depression, numbness, panic, irritability, or sadness can be a sign that extra support is needed. These symptoms may show up as racing thoughts, low motivation, tension, crying more often, avoiding people, or feeling disconnected from things you used to care about.

Individual counseling or anxiety and depression therapy can help you understand what is keeping the symptoms active and what kind of coping plan may help.

2. Relationships Keep Getting Pulled Into The Same Pattern

Therapy can help when conflict, distance, resentment, mistrust, or communication problems keep repeating. The concern may involve a partner, parent, child, sibling, co-parent, or the whole family system.

Depending on the situation, family therapy, couples counseling, or individual therapy may be the right starting point.

3. Coping Strategies Are Creating More Problems

People often look for relief when stress is high. If coping strategies are starting to create new problems, therapy may help.

That can include substance use, overeating or restriction, isolation, compulsive spending, self-harm, excessive screen use, or avoiding responsibilities because they feel too overwhelming.

4. Daily Life Feels Harder To Maintain

Mental health concerns can affect basic functioning. You may notice missed work or school, difficulty getting out of bed, poor sleep, trouble with hygiene, procrastination, avoidance, or feeling frozen by ordinary tasks.

When practical barriers are part of the problem, case management or community support may also be worth asking about.

5. A Major Change Or Trauma Is Still Affecting You

Divorce, grief, job loss, medical stress, relocation, family conflict, violence, or other traumatic experiences can leave the nervous system and relationships unsettled long after the event itself.

Therapy can provide a structured place to process what happened, rebuild stability, and decide what support is needed next.

Taking The Next Step

If several signs feel familiar, reaching out can help you clarify the right service instead of guessing alone. Louisville Mental Health Group offers support for individuals, children, families, couples, groups, and community-based needs. Start with what is happening now, and the next step can become clearer.

routes.postContent:
What to Expect When Starting Therapy

Starting therapy does not require having everything figured out before you call. Many people begin with a general sense that something needs to change, even if they are not sure which service, clinician, or care setting is the best fit.

The first step is usually a conversation about what is happening now, what kind of support may fit, and whether care should happen at the Louisville office, by telehealth, or in another appropriate setting.

What The First Contact Usually Covers

The office may ask about the main concern, the age of the client, insurance, scheduling needs, location, and whether there are safety, court, school, or family factors that should be understood early.

You do not need polished language. A practical description is enough: what changed, what feels hard to manage, and what kind of help you are hoping for.

What Happens After That

The goal is to identify a next step that is clear and manageable. That may be individual counseling, child counseling, family therapy, couples counseling, case management, community support, EMDR, or another service path.

If the first option is not the best fit, the conversation can still help narrow the direction.

Co-parenting services for steadier communication, child-focused coordination, parenting structure, and family stability.

What To Expect In Co-Parenting Support

Start with the communication pattern, parenting decisions, court expectations if any, and what the children are experiencing. That context helps clarify whether co-parenting support or another court-related service fits.

Child-focused Co-parenting

The goal is not forced agreement on everything. The work focuses on structure, communication, predictable routines, and reducing conflict that places children in the middle.

When Co-Parenting Support Helps

This service may be useful when communication breaks down easily, parenting decisions become conflict points, children are caught in the middle, or court-related expectations affect the family.

Communication and Coordination

We work with parents from Louisville, Jefferson County, Bullitt County, Oldham County, and nearby Southern Indiana who need a more stable way to manage parenting decisions after separation or conflict.

Does co-parenting support focus on the children's wellbeing?

Mental Health Compliance in Louisville, KY

Mental health compliance in Louisville, KY at LMHG includes privacy, licensing, ethics, billing, records, and contact questions.

Reach Out

HIPAA Compliance

We strictly adhere to the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) regulations. Your protected health information (PHI) is safeguarded through secure systems, confidential procedures, and staff training.

We do not disclose your information without your written consent, except as required by law or in emergency circumstances.

Privacy Practices

Your privacy is fundamental to the therapeutic relationship. We maintain confidential records and use secure communication methods. Our Notice of Privacy Practices describes how your information may be used and disclosed.

Licensing & Accreditation

Our clinicians are licensed professionals in the state of Kentucky. We maintain all required professional licenses and participate in ongoing professional development to meet licensure requirements.

Ethical Standards

We follow the ethical guidelines of our respective licensing boards and professional organizations. This includes maintaining appropriate professional boundaries, avoiding conflicts of interest, and prioritizing client welfare.

Insurance & Billing Compliance

We follow all applicable laws and regulations regarding insurance billing, including accurate coding and documentation. We work with most commercial insurance plans and Medicaid programs.

Questions About Compliance

If you have questions about our privacy practices, compliance standards, or your rights as a client, please contact us.

Questions About Compliance?

Contact the office if you have questions about privacy practices, compliance standards, records, or your rights as a client.

Therapy Cost in Louisville, KY

Answers about therapy cost in Louisville, KY, Medicaid, commercial insurance, private pay, coverage checks, and cost questions for LMHG mental health services.

Reach Out

Insurance And Medicaid

Louisville Mental Health Group commonly works with commercial insurance and Medicaid plans. Coverage can still vary by plan, service, clinician, documentation needs, and timing, so verification matters before care begins.

If you are unsure whether a service is covered, call the office or start through intake and include the insurance details you have available.

Private Pay

Private pay may be available when it is the best fit for the service, coverage situation, or client preference.

Because fees and availability can change, the most reliable next step is to contact the office before making assumptions about cost.

What To Ask Before Scheduling

Useful questions include whether the service is currently available, whether the provider accepts your plan, whether telehealth or in-person care fits, and whether a different service category would be a better starting point.

You do not need to solve every payment question alone before reaching out. The office can help clarify the practical next step.

Questions About This Page

Does LMHG accept insurance?

LMHG commonly works with commercial insurance and Medicaid plans, but coverage should be verified before care begins.

Can I ask about cost before scheduling?

Yes. Use intake or call the office with insurance details and the service you are considering so the team can help clarify next steps.

Louisville Mental Health Group Approach

The Louisville Mental Health Group approach helps clients find a practical starting point for therapy, case management, community support, and care settings.

Reach Out

Start With Fit

A useful starting point depends on the concern, the age of the client, family context, insurance, availability, and whether care should happen in the office, online, at home, at school, or in the community when appropriate.

The site is organized so visitors can begin with broad service categories or narrow in through specialties when the concern is already clear.

Support The Whole Situation

Mental health needs often connect to family stress, school pressure, relationships, court involvement, transportation, resources, routines, or follow-through between services.

That is why LMHG includes therapy and practical support options such as case management, community support, in-home care, and court-related family services.

Keep The Next Step Clear

The first contact does not have to be a final treatment decision. It can be a simple request for help identifying the right provider, service, setting, and timing.

Use the intake form or call the office with the main concern and any practical constraints. The team can help route the request from there.

Questions About This Page

How does LMHG help people choose a starting point?

The team considers the concern, family context, age, setting, insurance, and availability before helping identify the most practical next step.

Does care only happen in the office?

No. Depending on fit and availability, care may involve the Louisville office, telehealth, in-home support, school coordination, or community-based services.

Ready to Find the Next Step?

Use the intake form or call the office with the main concern and any practical constraints. The team can help route the request from there.

Reach Out

Group Therapy

Group therapy for structured support, guided discussion, shared learning, skill practice, and connection.

Reach Out

What To Expect In Group Therapy

Group therapy can help clients practice skills, reduce isolation, and hear from others while still being guided by a provider. Availability, fit, and group focus may vary, so the first step is talking with our team about what is currently offered.

Guided Discussion and Skills Practice

We support Louisville-area clients who may benefit from structured group care alongside or instead of individual, family, or other service paths when appropriate.

Related Services And Specialties

Group availability may change over time. Contact the office to confirm current options and whether a group setting is the right starting point.

Questions About This Page

Is group therapy a replacement for individual counseling?

Sometimes it can be a primary service, and sometimes it works better as an added support. Fit depends on goals, symptoms, schedule, and current group availability.

How do I know whether a group is available or appropriate?

Reach out to the office. The team can review current group options, basic fit, and whether another service should be considered first.

Is group therapy confidential?

Group therapy includes clear expectations for privacy and respect. A provider reviews group structure, participation expectations, and fit before someone joins.

Ask About Current Groups

If group therapy sounds like it may fit, contact the office so we can review availability and next steps.

Individual Counseling

Individual counseling for adults and teens facing anxiety, depression, trauma, stress, relationship strain, or life changes.

Reach Out

What To Expect In Individual Therapy

Our individual counseling work is built around practical support, consistent care, and treatment that adapts to what is happening in your life right now. We focus on helping clients feel more stable, more clear, and more able to move forward.

Anxiety, Depression, Trauma, and Stress

We work with adults and adolescents across Louisville neighborhoods, Jefferson County, Bullitt County, and Oldham County, with telehealth and office-based options considered when appropriate.

Related Services And Specialties

These focused pages can help if you are comparing adult counseling with anxiety and depression therapy or another individual support path.

Adult Counseling

Adult counseling for stress, anxiety, depression, grief, burnout, relationship strain, trauma, and major life changes.

What concerns do you address in individual counseling?

Individual counseling commonly supports anxiety, depression, trauma, grief, stress, relationship strain, emotional overwhelm, and life transitions.

Do I need to know exactly what kind of therapy I need?

No. You can start by describing what feels hardest to manage, and the office can help identify whether individual counseling or another LMHG service is the right first step.

Call the office or use the intake form. You can share what is happening, ask about insurance, and talk through whether individual counseling is the right starting point.

What To Expect In Parenting Support

Start with what feels hardest at home, what your child or teen is struggling with, and whether caregiver guidance, family therapy, or child counseling seems closest to the need.

Parenting Help

Parenting stress often grows when behavior, emotions, school demands, and family routines all collide. Support can help caregivers identify what is driving conflict and choose responses that are more consistent.

Who Parenting Support Is For

This service may fit when caregivers are managing behavior challenges, emotional regulation problems, transition stress, co-parenting strain, or a home environment that feels tense and hard to stabilize.

Behavior and Boundaries

Parents from Louisville, Jefferson County, Bullitt County, Oldham County, and nearby Southern Indiana often reach out when work pressure, school stress, and family conflict make home feel harder to manage.

Can parenting support help if my child is also in therapy?

Yes. Families often benefit when caregiver support and child-focused therapy reinforce each other rather than operating separately.

Does parenting support include specific behavior plans?

It can. The work may include communication strategies, structure, co-regulation, boundaries, and response planning shaped around your family.

Is parenting support for the parent, the child, or both?

It depends on the concern. Parenting support may focus on caregiver responses, the child's needs, family routines, or how everyone interacts at home.

Play therapy for children who need developmentally appropriate support for feelings, behavior, stress, trauma, and family change.

What To Expect In Play Therapy

Start with what you are seeing at home, school, bedtime, transitions, or family routines. That helps the office understand whether play therapy, child counseling, behavioral support, or family therapy is the best first step.

Play-based Support for Children

Children do not always have adult words for what they feel or need. Play therapy uses structured therapeutic play, relationship, and age-appropriate expression to help children work through emotions, stress, behavior patterns, or difficult experiences.

When Play Therapy May Fit

Play therapy may help when a child is struggling with big feelings, behavior changes, anxiety, trauma, grief, family transitions, school stress, or experiences they cannot easily explain in conversation.

Child Counseling With Caregiver Context

Families across Louisville, Jefferson County, Bullitt County, and Oldham County often ask about play therapy when younger children need support and caregivers need practical direction for home, school, and routines.

Questions About This Page

What is play therapy?

Play therapy is a child-focused therapy approach that uses therapeutic play and relationship to help children express feelings, practice coping, and process experiences in a developmentally appropriate way.

Are caregivers involved in play therapy?

Often, yes. Caregiver involvement helps connect session work with emotional regulation, routines, communication, and follow-through outside therapy.

How is play therapy related to child counseling?

Play therapy is one child counseling path. It may be the clearest fit for younger children, while teen counseling, child behavioral support, or family therapy may fit other needs.

Relationship counseling looks at communication loops, trust ruptures, emotional misunderstandings, and practical pressures that leave partners feeling stuck.

When Relationship Counseling Helps

This service may fit when couples feel disconnected, argue repeatedly, struggle to repair after conflict, or need a more stable way to talk through difficult issues.

Is relationship counseling only for married couples?

No. It can support married, engaged, long-term, or dating couples who want help improving the relationship.

Will therapy force us to stay together?

What if communication has already broken down?

Relationship counseling can help slow the pattern down, clarify what keeps going wrong, and build a more structured way to speak and listen.

No. It can support married, engaged, long-term, or dating couples who want help improving the relationship.
Will therapy force us to stay together?
No. The goal is clarity, healthier communication, and a more thoughtful process for moving forward, whatever that ultimately looks like.
What if communication has already broken down?
Relationship counseling can help slow the pattern down, clarify what keeps going wrong, and build a more structured way to speak and listen.
Use the intake form or call (502) 416-1416 to ask about relationship counseling, provider fit, availability, and whether another couples service fits better.

How do I choose the right mental health service?

Start with the broad service category that best matches the main concern, such as individual counseling, child counseling, family therapy, couples counseling, court-ordered services, community-based support, group therapy, or trauma therapy. Is therapy in your home the same as case management?

No. Home-based therapy brings clinical support into the home, while case management focuses more broadly on coordination and practical resource support.

Can home-based services reduce transportation barriers?

Yes. Access barriers are one reason families sometimes ask about home-based options.

Is home-based therapy available everywhere?

routes.postContent:
Trauma Therapy

Trauma therapy for adults, teens, and families working through distressing memories, triggers, grief, anxiety, and PTSD symptoms.

Reach Out

What To Expect In Trauma Therapy

Trauma therapy gives clients a steadier place to work with experiences that still affect mood, sleep, relationships, daily routines, or a sense of safety. Care may include trauma-focused talk therapy, EMDR when appropriate, and support that fits the person's age, symptoms, and family context.

Trauma-informed Support

We support Louisville-area adults, teens, and families who may be dealing with trauma responses at home, school, work, or in relationships. Care can connect with individual counseling, child counseling, family therapy, or EMDR when those paths fit.

Related Services And Specialties

Start here for broad trauma therapy. Use EMDR therapy when that specific modality is already in view, or individual, child, and family pages when the concern connects to a wider care context.

EMDR Therapy in Louisville, KY

keyword-map

View Page

Questions About This Page

What can trauma therapy help with?

Trauma therapy may help with distressing memories, triggers, avoidance, anxiety, grief, relationship strain, emotional shutdown, or feeling on edge after difficult experiences.

Is EMDR part of trauma therapy?

EMDR is one trauma-focused therapy option. It may fit some clients, while others may start with individual counseling, family support, or another trauma-informed care path.

Do I need to know exactly what kind of trauma therapy I need?

No. You can start by describing what is happening now, what still feels difficult, and whether you are asking for yourself, a child, or a family member.

Start With What Still Feels Active

You do not need to explain everything perfectly before reaching out. Share the current impact, and the office can help route the request toward trauma therapy, EMDR, or another connected service.
